title: Asking for help with improving simulation performance
body: Hi, thank you for supporting this amazing tool. I'd like to ask for help with troubleshooting simulation performance issues. I have a large RTL design (ARM interconnect) and a set of small testbenches to compare simulation performance between VCS and Verilator. The problem is that verilated model on 32 threads is approximately 2.5 times worse than VCS simulation (walltime ~8210s vs ~3180s) and I don't have any more ideas how to fix it. From my experience single-threaded verilated model performance should be comparable with VCS one, so I focused on optimizing it before introducing multithreading. Everything below is about single-threaded verilated models. I'll provide numbers from one specific test beacuse as far as I've seen all tests show approximately the same results. `perf stat` (5 runs) for VCS simulation (binary size: ~1MB): `perf stat` (5 runs) for verilated model (the best one, ~14 times worse than VCS, binary size: ~130M): Used Verilator options in this test: ``` --cc --sv +1800-2017ext+sv +1800-2017ext+v -Wno-fatal -error-limit 1000 --timescale-override 1ns/1ps --x-assign fast --x-initial fast --x-initial-edge --verilate-jobs 64 -O3 --main --timing --no-assert -CFLAGS -DPRINT_INFO -CFLAGS -DPRINT_ERROR -CFLAGS -fcoroutines -CFLAGS -static -CFLAGS -static-libstdc++ -CFLAGS -flto=auto -LDFLAGS -static -LDFLAGS -static-libstdc++ -LDFLAGS -flto=auto +define+UNROLL_LOOP +define+MANUAL_ISOLATE_ASSIGNMENTS +define+VERILATOR_MANUAL_PATCH ``` The last defines are used for patching to fix verilation warnings (see below). Not default C++ compiling options: ``` OPT=-march=native -mtune=native -fcoroutines -static -static-libstdc++ -flto=auto ``` As you can see from verilation options there is no custom C++ wrapper (--main) and the testbench is fully written in SV (and so it requires --timing). The RTL design is patched to fix UNOPTFLAT and MULTIDRIVEN warnings. Only the following verilation warnings occur: One of patches is essentially manual `for` loop unrolling in order to let verilator split huge multidimensional arrays (otherwise Verilator says `index can not be determined statically`, doesn't manage to split these variables, which leads to UNOPTFLAT warnings). I tried the following changes in verilation options: - Changing verilation -O3 to other -O leads to performance degradation. - Turning off patches (resulting in UNOPTFLAT and MULTIDRIVEN warnings) makes performance 2-3 times worse (I can get specific numbers if needed). - Turning off LTO/static linking leads to small performance degradation. I tried investigating why there is 43% branch misses in verilated model simulation (see earlier) using perf record. If I understood correctly there is no one specific 'hot' place both in terms of cycles and branch misses, it is spread quite evenly over dozens of C++ functions. One of many examples of Verilator surpassing VCS in simulation performance I know of is open-source RISC-V core SCR1 (https://github.com/syntacore/scr1). It supports Verilator simulation out of the box, I ran a heavy Coremark testbench with single-threaded verilated model on the same machine as the earlier tests. Verilator finished in ~3 hours, VCS didn't manage to finish in ~30 hours. Used verilation options are quite simple. The main difference is that they use --no-timing and a simple custom C++ wrapper. VCS binary's size for SCR1 is ~800KB, verilated model binary is ~450KB. I also have a dummy, which replaces original RTL design but all the rest is the same (testbenches, Verilator & VCS options and so on). It is interesting that it was not easy to find a combination which resulted in Verilator surpassing VCS in single thread. The possible reasons are: - this dummy is too simple; - something's wrong with testbenches and/or building system leading to bad verilated model performance. Here are some results for it: `perf stat` (5 runs) for VCS simulation (binary size: ~800KB) `perf stat` (5 runs) for verilated model (--timing, full SV testbench, binary size: ~4.9MB): Full list of options: ``` --cc --sv +1800-2017ext+sv +1800-2017ext+v -Wno-fatal -error-limit 1000 --timescale-override 1ns/1ps --x-assign fast --x-initial fast --x-initial-edge --verilate-jobs 64 -O3 --main --timing --no-assert -CFLAGS -DPRINT_INFO -CFLAGS -DPRINT_ERROR -CFLAGS -std=c++20 -CFLAGS -flto=auto -LDFLAGS -static -LDFLAGS -static-libstdc++ -LDFLAGS -flto=auto +define+UNROLL_LOOP +define+MANUAL_ISOLATE_ASSIGNMENTS +define+VERILATOR_MANUAL_PATCH CXX=clang++ LINK=clang++ OPT=-march=native -mtune=native -fuse-ld=lld -static -static-libstdc++ -flto=auto ``` `perf stat` (5 runs) for verilated model (--no-timing, full C++ wrapper testbench, binary size: ~4.7MB): Full list of options: ``` --cc --sv +1800-2017ext+sv +1800-2017ext+v -Wno-fatal -error-limit 1000 --timescale-override 1ns/1ps --x-assign fast --x-initial fast --x-initial-edge --verilate-jobs 64 -O3 --no-timing --no-assert -CFLAGS -DPRINT_INFO -CFLAGS -DPRINT_ERROR -CFLAGS -std=c++20 -CFLAGS -flto=auto -LDFLAGS -static -LDFLAGS -static-libstdc++ -LDFLAGS -flto=auto +define+UNROLL_LOOP +define+MANUAL_ISOLATE_ASSIGNMENTS +define+VERILATOR_MANUAL_PATCH CXX=clang++ LINK=clang++ OPT=-march=native -mtune=native -fuse-ld=lld -static -static-libstdc++ -flto=auto ``` I'm attaching an [archive](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25099770/for_verilator_issue.zip) containing the dummy design, the testbench and makefile used to verilate and build it. The testbench and the makefile are identical to those used with the real design. I admit that the root of the problem may be in them and not in the design itself. Although in case of dummy using full C++ testbench (--no-timing) helps to have a good performance, it doesn't change much in case of the original design. Here is a perf report for simulation run of the original design (--no-timing, full C++ wrapper testbench): As far as I understand this report the way my C++ wrapper is written doesn't cause the performance to degrade since no of its functions are high in the list (moreover, it's structurally the same wrapper as one used with the dummy). Is there something obvious I miss? I'll greatly appreciate any help and suggestions on what else I can try to do to get a faster verilated model simulation than VCS one (single-thread or, if it's wrong way trying to optimise single-thread first, mulit-thread). Verilator: 5.042 g++ (when used by Verilator): 12.2.1 clang++: 19.1.7
